judgments.title: WAYTHAMOORTHY A/L PONNUSAMY v PENERBITAN SAHABAT (M) SDN BHD & ors
judgments.catchwords: TORT: Defamation — Libel — Newspaper article — Tamil daily — Article portraying Plaintiff as a "saboteur" attempting to stop the Thaipusam festival — Whether words in their natural and ordinary meaning defamatory — Whether words referred to Plaintiff — Whether words published to third parties — Translation dispute — Meaning of Tamil word "Vettu" — Whether interpretations of "sabotage", "obstruct", or "incite" defamatory per se — "Defamatory half-truth" — Selective editing of Plaintiff's open letter to suppress material context of public health concerns — Whether Article constituted an attack on Plaintiff's moral character. TORT: Defamation — Defences — Fair Comment — Requirements — Whether comment based on facts — Editorial invention of "saboteur" narrative — Whether characterisation of motives as political had factual foundation — Whether defeated by express malice — Reckless indifference to truth. TORT: Defamation — Defences — Qualified Privilege — Common and corresponding interest — Whether defeated by express malice — Particulars of malice — Rules of Court 2012, Order 78 Rule 3(3) — Creation of derogatory pseudonym "Vettu Vaikkum Waytha Moorthy" — Use of large red fonts to signal targeted campaign — Failure to retract or apologise despite Letter of Demand. TORT: Defamation — Defences — Reynolds Privilege — Responsible journalism — Factors to consider — Seriousness of allegation — Nature of information — Source of information — Steps taken to verify — Urgency — Whether comment sought from Plaintiff — Whether article contained "gist" of Plaintiff’s side of story — Failure to include public health context of open letter — Lack of verifiable evidence of alleged "nationwide condemnation". CIVIL PROCEDURE: Pleadings — Defamation — Adequacy of Statement of Claim — Failure to identify precise words complained of — Verbatim reproduction of entire article — Whether Defendants caught off-guard or prejudiced — Whether technical objections sustainable at close of trial. EVIDENCE: Adverse inference — Evidence Act 1950, s. 114(g) — Failure to call temple administrators or produce reporter notes to substantiate claim of public anger — Whether adverse inference drawn against Defendants. DAMAGES: Assessment — Public figure — Former Federal Minister and Senator — Position and standing — Gravity of "sting" — Allegations of being a traitor to the Hindu community — Mode and extent of publication — Conduct of Defendants — Lack of remorse — General damages — Aggravated damages. REMEDIES: Injunction — Restraining publication already in public domain — Whether exercise in futility. REMEDIES: Mandatory apology — Whether Court has jurisdiction to compel a defendant to publish an apology — Absence of statutory or common law foundation — Apology as a voluntary act — Compelled speech — Whether Mandatory Order for Rectification and Factual Withdrawal available under inherent jurisdiction.
judgments.judge: YA Tuan Avinder Singh Gill a/l Ranjit Singh

judgments.title: VIGILANT VENTURES SDN BHD v Lembaga Kemajuan Ikan Malaysia (LKIM)
judgments.catchwords: Contract Law – Formation and interpretation – Collaboration Agreement – Duration clause – Intention for 30 years subject to detailed extension mechanism – Trial period and automatic extensions – Further extensions require mutual written agreement – No unilateral right to compel extension after second automatic extension Contract Law – Expiry by effluxion of time – Distinguished from termination for breach – Agreement expiring by its own terms does not require notice of default or termination – Contract simply comes to an end Contract Law – Fraud and misrepresentation – Contract induced by fraud remains valid until rescinded – Finding of fraud relevant to damages, not to contractual expiry – Not contradictory to finding that contract was lawfully entered into Contract Law – Damages – Special damages – Must be strictly pleaded and strictly proved – Supported by documentary evidence (invoices, receipts, accounts) – Loss of guaranteed profits – Failure to prove actual gross income – Award set aside Contract Law – Damages – General damages – Global award of RM2.4 million – No proper legal basis pleaded (account of profits/unjust enrichment not pleaded) – No strict proof of actual loss – Award set aside Contract Law – Damages – Election between reliance loss and expectation loss – Claimant must elect – Cannot claim both Land Law – Vacant possession – Holding over after expiry of agreement – Registered proprietor entitled to vacant possession – Order affirmed

parties.label: PIHAK KETIGA
names: DATUK BANDAR, DEWAN BANDARAYA KUALA LUMPUR

judgments.catchwords: "Full trial of consolidated suits by Management Corporation and Purchasers of condominium units seeking to declare a Sale and Purchase agreement between Developer and another as void and to transfer what they consider common property to the Management Committee. - Issues interalia are whether fraud has been established and the registered title to the property indefeasible; whether the property and accessory car parks constitute common property; whether the Developer has breached the SPA's; whether the MC has the requisite locus standi; and whether the Defendants counter claim for tresspass proven? - Court makes the following findings :- 1. the MC lacks the requisite locus standi but the Purchases have so; 2. the Plaintiffs have failed to establish fraud; 3. the property is not common property as determined by reference to the Certified Strata Plans and Schedule of Parcels; 4. no breach of the DOs or SPA's have been establish in that there is no shortage of car parks; 5. counter claim dismissed as no evidence of tresspass. Plaintiffs claims in both suits dismissed.

judgments.catchwords: Construction Industry Payment and Adjudication Act 2012 – a question of considerable Doctrinal importance at the intersection of construction law and insolvency law: what is the effect, upon a winding-up order against a company which was founded upon an adjudication decision obtained by that company’s creditor, of a subsequent final determination by the court which conclusively reverses that adjudication decision on the merits - does the winding up order — by itself, and notwithstanding that the adjudication decision upon which it was founded has been extinguished under s. 13(c) of CIPAA by the final court determination — have the effect of transmuting the adjudication decision into a binding, permanent debt which survives that final determination, and which the CIPAA claimant may thereafter invoke as a mutual set-off under s. 526 of the Companies Act 2016 (“CA 2016”) against a winding-up petition subsequently presented by the aggrieved debtor company whose claims were vindicated in those very court proceedings? - the meaning of “finally decided by arbitration or the court” in s. 13(c), whether final determination means the decision must have been “decided, and no longer open to any form of challenge or appeal.
judgments.judge: YA Dato' Ong Chee Kwan
coram: YA Datuk Seri Mohd Firuz Bin Jaffril

judgments.title: Chung Soh Eng v Chung Yang Huang & ors
judgments.catchwords: This dispute does not spring from commercial rivalry or contractual missteps, but from a family once held together by shared blood and memory, now strained by grief, mistrust, and differing recollections of what the Deceased may have wished. The Court is mindful that the Deceased was remembered as a gentle anchor in the family an elder sibling whose presence steadied the others. Yet the law governing insurance nominations is not a sail that shifts with the winds of sentiment; it is the keel of the vessel, fixed and steady, guiding the course regardless of emotional tides. In resolving this conflict, the Court’s task is not to weigh the hearts of siblings or to choose between competing narratives, but to ensure that the estate is administered according to law—fairly, transparently, and with the quiet dignity that the Deceased herself would have deserved. Orders For the reasons above, the Plaintiff’s Amended Originating Summons (Encl. 29) is allowed. Accordingly, the Court orders: (i) Declarations that all proceeds from HLA 4552, the nine AIA Life Policies, the AIA Compassionate Allowance Policy, and the Manulife Policy form part of the Deceased’s estate and are held on trust by the respective Defendants; (ii) Orders that the Defendants pay the said proceeds to the estate within 14 days upon expiry of the 30 days ( filing of the affidavit); (iii) Orders for accounts and disclosure by way of affidavit within 30 days from the date of the order, to include all relevant amounts, dates, and supporting documents. (iv) No Order as to interest; (v) Costs in the cause.
judgments.judge: YA Tuan Nixon Anak Kennedy Kumbong

judgments.title: MANJULA A/P MANIKAM v SHARMILA A/P NAGESWARAN
judgments.catchwords: This case concerns an application to strike out the Plaintiff's suit challenging the validity of her late father's Will. The Plaintiff alleged that the Will was forged, that her father lacked testamentary capacity when he executed it, and that suspicious circumstances surrounded its making and the grant of probate obtained by the Defendant, her sister-in-law and the appointed executrix. The Court found all three grounds unsustainable: the forgery allegation lacked the particulars required under O 18 R 7(1) ROC 2012 and any supporting expert evidence under s 45(1) of the Evidence Act 1950; the incapacity allegation lacked medical evidence and was contradicted by uncontroverted proof of the deceased's mental soundness; and the suspicious-circumstances argument also failed because the Defendant held no beneficial interest under the Will. Applying Bandar Builder and Serac Asia, the Court held the claim, at its highest, disclosed no triable issue and allowed the striking-out application with costs. Striking out of pleadings; obviously unsustainable claim; testamentary capacity; allegation of forgery; particulars of fraud; suspicious circumstances; grant of probate; scandalous pleadings; Order 18 ROC 2012; Evidence Act 1950.
judgments.judge: YA Puan Arziah binti Mohamed Apandi

judgments.title: Pendakwa Raya v DEVABALAN A/L MARIAPPAN
judgments.catchwords: Criminal trial - the Accused pleaded guilty to the alternative charge in BA-45A-84-09/2025 and the charge in BA-45-39-09/2025 Mitigation - the Accused submits that a lenient sentence is appropriate and warranted in this matter, as he has pleaded guilty to all charges at the earliest available opportunity, no previous convictions, repentant, is now 31 years of age with strong prospects for rehabilitation and the weight of the illicit substances involved is not substantial - learned DPP, on the other hand, vehemently submits that an exemplary and deterrent sentence must be imposed upon the Accused - the public interest and deterrence are still the overriding consideration as well as the drugs involved are classified as highly potent and hardcore narcotics. Sentence - this Court is mindful that the Accused has pleaded guilty to all charges at the earliest available opportunity - this action not only demonstrates his immediate acceptance of responsibility but has saved valuable judicial time and public resources by avoiding a protracted trial - the plea of guilty of the Accused stands as a primary mitigating factor - the Accused also is a first-time offender which reflects that he is not a hardened or habitual criminal - nevertheless, the above mitigating factors do not absolve this Court from its duty to uphold the public interest and apply the necessary elements of both general and specific deterrence - a balanced approach is paramount - illicit drugs remain the prime national enemy, inflicting severe socio-economic harm upon society - a lenient sentence in this matter would fail to reflect the gravity of the offence and undermine the Government’s strict stance and unwavering policy in combatting this critical menace - the present case involves drugs which are classified as highly potent and hardcore narcotics - possess an exceptionally high risk of addiction and are notorious for causing catastrophic, irreversible physical and psychological destruction to users, thereby posing a severe threat to public health - the Accused’s criminal conduct extended far beyond drug possession - in attempting to evade arrest, the Accused exhibited a total disregard for public safety, causing extensive property damage to both police vehicle and multiple civilian vehicles - this reckless behaviour endangered innocent lives and significantly exacerbates the gravity of the offence.
judgments.judge: YA Dato' Sri Shamsulbahri bin Haji Ibrahim

judgments.title: NOMALAN A/L RAMAYAH v Pendakwa Raya
judgments.catchwords: Abolition of Mandatory Death Penalty Act 2023 – Child sentence under Section 97(2) of the Child Act 2001- "detained in prison during the pleasure of the Yang di-Pertuan Agong (King) or Ruler"- does not violate Article 5 because the child offender is convicted after the due process of a trial and at its conclusion, the sentence passed is in accordance with a validly passed law- Doctrine of reasonable classification- doctrine of proportionality-ultimate objective was to place child offenders in a category of their own so as to not suffer the extreme fate of adult offenders with the view to being given an opportunity at rehabilitation and their eventual re-integration back into society- reasonable nexus that existed between the discrimination and that legislative aim sought to be achieved-no violation or fundamental breach of Article 8-alternative remedies provided for under the law for the Applicants to be dealt with according to Section 97(4) of the Child Act 2001, Regulation 54 and 113 of the Prison Regulations 2000 read together with power of pardon under Article 42 of the Federal Constitution-remedy afforded exclusively to the Applicants via section 97(5) of the Child Act 2001 provides the opportunity for them to be released earlier from detention, by virtue of the yearly recommendation by the Board of Visiting Justices. This opportunity is not afforded to a convict of the age of majority- Section 2 and Section 3 of Act 847 makes in clear that the pre-condition for the application under Section 3 is that the person must be on death row at the date of enforcement of Act 847- Applicants’ squarely outside the purview of Act 847

judgments.title: MUHAMAD HAFIZUL HAFIQ BIN MASRI v Pendakwa Raya
judgments.catchwords: Abolition of Mandatory Death Penalty Act 2023 – Child sentence under Section 97(2) of the Child Act 2001- "detained in prison during the pleasure of the Yang di-Pertuan Agong (King) or Ruler"- does not violate Article 5 because the child offender is convicted after the due process of a trial and at its conclusion, the sentence passed is in accordance with a validly passed law- Doctrine of reasonable classification- doctrine of proportionality-ultimate objective was to place child offenders in a category of their own so as to not suffer the extreme fate of adult offenders with the view to being given an opportunity at rehabilitation and their eventual re-integration back into society- reasonable nexus that existed between the discrimination and that legislative aim sought to be achieved-no violation or fundamental breach of Article 8-alternative remedies provided for under the law for the Applicants to be dealt with according to Section 97(4) of the Child Act 2001, Regulation 54 and 113 of the Prison Regulations 2000 read together with power of pardon under Article 42 of the Federal Constitution-remedy afforded exclusively to the Applicants via section 97(5) of the Child Act 2001 provides the opportunity for them to be released earlier from detention, by virtue of the yearly recommendation by the Board of Visiting Justices. This opportunity is not afforded to a convict of the age of majority- Section 2 and Section 3 of Act 847 makes in clear that the pre-condition for the application under Section 3 is that the person must be on death row at the date of enforcement of Act 847- Applicants’ squarely outside the purview of Act 847

judgments.title: KAMALESWARAN THAVER A/L SUPPIAH v Pendakwa Raya & ors
judgments.catchwords: Prosedur Jenayah — Rayuan terhadap sabitan dan hukuman — Tertuduh didakwa mengedar dadah berbahaya di bawah s 39B(1)(a) Akta Dadah Berbahaya 1952 (ADB) - boleh dihukum di bawah s 39B (2) ADB — Sama ada pembelaan dipertimbangkan secukupnya oleh Hakim Perbicaraan — Sama ada hakim perbicaraan terkhilaf dalam keputusan – Mahkamah ini mengurangkan pertuduhan kepada pertuduhan di bawah seksyen 12(2) ADB, dan boleh dihukum di bawah s 39A(2) ADB - Tertuduh dijatuhkan hukuman pemenjaraan selama 10 tahun dari tarikh tangkapan dan 10 kali sebatan rotan.
